did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically an ensemble of four Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures (ResNet50, EfficientNet B6, InceptionV3, and Xception) combined with a stacking ensemble learning approach, to classify melanoma skin cancer from ISIC dataset images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and analysis of an improved deep ensemble learning model for melanoma classification. It details the development of a balanced dataset from ISIC sources, the training of specific CNN architectures (ResNet50, EfficientNet B6, InceptionV3, Xception) using a stacking ensemble approach, and reports new experimental results including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICACS'23), it contains original algorithmic contributions and validation results rather than merely summarizing prior literature.

title: Design and Analysis of an Improved Deep Ensemble Learning Model for Melanoma Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2023 4th International Conference on Advancements in Computational Sciences (ICACS)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma skin cancer classification by developing an improved deep ensemble learning model to assist dermatologists in accurate diagnosis. The researchers constructed a balanced dataset of 10,500 images from multiple publicly available ISIC datasets, which was used to train an ensemble of four pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network architectures: ResNet50, EfficientNet B6, InceptionV3, and Xception. These base learners were combined using a stacking ensemble approach with logistic regression for final classification. The experimental results demonstrated that the proposed stacking ensemble model outperformed individual base learners and other state-of-the-art methods in classifying melanoma skin cancer. The study concludes that this deep ensemble technique provides satisfactory and superior diagnostic accuracy compared to existing approaches.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification. The study constructs a balanced dataset from ISIC sources to classify melanoma skin cancer. In the confusion matrix analysis, it is referred to as the 'malignant class' with 551 true positives.

paper_terms: Benign melanoma class of skin cancer
lesion_types_studied.details: The negative class in the binary classification task. The dataset includes benign skin moles and diseases. In the confusion matrix analysis, it is referred to as the 'benign class' with 484 true negatives.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'Malignant' (Melanoma) and 'Benign' classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'classification of melanoma skin cancer' using a stacking ensemble of CNNs (ResNet50, EfficientNet B6, InceptionV3, Xception) with logistic regression for final classification. The target labels are defined as Y = {y/y ∈ {Benign, Malignant}}. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix (TP, TN, FP, FN), confirming a discrete diagnostic category prediction task.

datasets_used.name: Custom Balanced ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A newly constructed balanced dataset created by the authors by combining images from multiple publicly available ISIC datasets (ISIC 2019, ISIC 2018) and the HAM10000 dataset. Data augmentation (up-sampling) was applied to the malignant class to address class imbalance. Images were resized to 300x300 pixels.

use.training: Used to train the four base CNN models (ResNet50, EfficientNet B6, InceptionV3, Xception) for 30 epochs with mini-batches.
use.validation: Used for early stopping criteria (monitoring validation accuracy for five epochs).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final stacking ensemble model's performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices.

Custom Balanced ISIC Dataset.description: A balanced dataset of 10,600 images (9,500 training, 1,100 testing) constructed from ISIC 2019, ISIC 2018, and HAM10000 datasets. Classes: Benign (4,500 images) and Malignant (5,000 images). Images resized to 300x300 pixels.
Custom Balanced ISIC Dataset.evaluation_setting: Binary classification of Melanoma (Malignant) vs. Benign skin lesions using a stacking ensemble of ResNet50, EfficientNet B6, InceptionV3, and Xception with Logistic Regression as the meta-learner.

Proposed Stacking Ensemble Model.notes: The model outperformed individual base learners. The text states 'All of the models on the dataset had more than 90% accuracy' for the base learners, but specific metrics for individual base learners are not explicitly listed in the results section, only a comparative statement.

ResNet50.notes: Specific precision, recall, and confusion matrix values for ResNet50 alone were not reported in the text, only accuracy and F1-score.

InceptionV3.notes: Included in the ensemble. The text states all base models had >90% accuracy and were outperformed by the ensemble, but specific metrics for InceptionV3 alone are not provided.

EfficientNet B6.notes: Included in the ensemble. The text states all base models had >90% accuracy and were outperformed by the ensemble, but specific metrics for EfficientNet B6 alone are not provided.

Xception.notes: Included in the ensemble. The text states all base models had >90% accuracy and were outperformed by the ensemble, but specific metrics for Xception alone are not provided.

Custom Balanced ISIC Dataset.notes: The proposed stacking ensemble model achieved 90% accuracy. It correctly classified 551 malignant images (TP) and 484 benign images (TN). It misclassified 55 malignant images as benign (FN) and 16 benign images as malignant (FP). The ensemble outperformed the individual base learners (ResNet50, InceptionV3, EfficientNet B6, Xception).

architecture_used.main_model: Stacking Ensemble of four pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (ResNet50, EfficientNet B6, InceptionV3, Xception) with Logistic Regression as the meta-learner
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet (implied by 'state-of-the-art' and 'transfer learning' context for these specific architectures)

base_learners.name: ResNet50
base_learners.details: 50-layer CNN with 48 convolutional layers, average pooling, max pool layer, and shortcut connections to handle vanishing gradients.

base_learners.name: EfficientNet B6
base_learners.details: Pre-trained CNN architecture noted for excellent accuracy on ImageNet with minimal computational cost.

base_learners.name: InceptionV3
base_learners.details: 48-layer architecture using building blocks of convolution, pooling, dropout, and fully connected layers with batch normalization.

base_learners.name: Xception
base_learners.details: Feature extraction component made of 36 convolutional layers divided into 14 modules, with linear skip connections.

meta_learner.name: Logistic Regression
meta_learner.details: Used for final binary classification. Input from base learners saved in NumPy array. Employs sigmoid activation function.

training_details.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy (for base learners); Softmax mentioned for cost calculation in text but Logistic Regression/Sigmoid used for final meta-learner
training_details.early_stopping: Stop training if validation accuracy does not improve for five epochs
activation_functions: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) for base learners

classification_or_output_head.details: Combines outputs of all four base models and feeds them into logistic regression for final binary classification (Benign vs. Malignant).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Stacking Ensemble Model (with Logistic Regression meta-learner)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 300 x 300 pixels using Python OpenCV library
Data augmentation (up-sampling) applied to the malignant class to address class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the malignant class to address class imbalance in the dataset. The malignant class was augmented to match the size of the benign class, resulting in a balanced dataset of 10,600 images (4,500 benign, 5,000 malignant).

Mentioned in Algorithm 1 as a step performed on images to tackle the overfitting problem, but specific geometric or color transform techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping, scaling) and their parameters are not explicitly detailed in the provided text.

conclusions: The proposed deep ensemble learning model, which combines ResNet50, EfficientNet B6, InceptionV3, and Xception using a stacking approach with logistic regression, correctly classifies melanoma skin cancer.
The proposed stacking ensemble model outperformed the individual base CNN learners (ResNet50, InceptionV3, EfficientNet B6, and Xception) in terms of diagnostic performance.
The model achieved an overall accuracy of 0.90 (90%) on the balanced dataset.
For the malignant class, the model achieved a precision of 0.97 and a recall of 0.90.
For the benign class, the model achieved a precision of 0.89 and a recall of 0.97.

The confusion matrix analysis showed that the model correctly identified 551 true positives (malignant) and 484 true negatives (benign), while misclassifying 55 malignant cases as benign and 16 benign cases as malignant.
All individual base models in the study achieved more than 90% accuracy, but the stacked ensemble model provided superior results compared to these individual architectures.

The study utilized a balanced dataset of 10,500 images constructed from multiple ISIC datasets to address class imbalance, which contributed to the model's satisfactory results compared to other state-of-the-art methods.
important_remarks: The study constructs a custom balanced dataset by combining ISIC 2019, ISIC 2018, and HAM10000 datasets, which may introduce dataset bias and limits direct comparability with studies using standard, held-out test splits of these public datasets.

Data augmentation was limited to up-sampling the malignant class to balance the dataset; specific geometric or color transform augmentations are not detailed, potentially limiting the model's robustness to real-world variations.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between the proposed ensemble and baseline models.

Specific performance metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) for individual base learners (InceptionV3, EfficientNet B6, Xception) are not explicitly reported, preventing independent verification of their individual contributions.

The dataset size (10,600 images) is relatively small for deep learning, and the reliance on transfer learning from pre-trained models (ImageNet) means the model may not generalize well to out-of-distribution data without further fine-tuning.
The study is published in a conference proceedings (ICACS'23) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.
No external validation on a completely independent dataset is reported; the model is only evaluated on the internal test split of the constructed dataset, raising concerns about generalizability.

The paper claims the model 'outperforms' other state-of-the-art methods but does not provide a detailed comparison table with specific metrics from those cited works, making the claim difficult to substantiate.
